disambiguation_rules: lai + diye/deko = outgoing payment or goods
lai + becheko = sale (credit possible if no payment verb follows)
lai + tireko = definite payment out
lai before person = recipient; lai before item = object marker (colloquial)
X lai Y bhayo = Y happened to X (not a transfer)
common_confusions: lai vs le: 'Ram lai' = to Ram (recipient); 'Ram le' = by Ram (agent)
colloquially lai sometimes dropped: 'Ram deko' still means 'gave to Ram'
lai after non-person (pasal lai) = for the shop

disambiguation_rules: le + action verb = agent who performed action
instrument le = payment method (cash le, cheque le, online le)
le identifies the payer/seller/giver — not the receiver
le vs lai: 'Ram le deko' = Ram gave; 'Ram lai deko' = gave to Ram
common_confusions: le often dropped in speech: 'Ram tiryo' = 'Ram le tiryo'
le after instrument mistaken for agent: 'card le' = by card not 'card paid'
colloquial merger: 'usle' = 'usley' (he/she did it)

disambiguation_rules: bata + aayo/paayo = inbound receipt
bata + jhikeko = withdrawal from named source
bata + kineko = purchase (source = supplier)
bata + kateko = deduction (negative from named source)
bata marks source; opposite direction to lai
common_confusions: bata vs dekhi: bata = physical source; dekhi = time-from or range-start
baata/bat are common short spellings
'usle bata leko' = took from him (bata = source, le = agent)

example_patterns.meaning: money is with X / X holds balance

disambiguation_rules: sanga + hisab = account reconciliation signal
sanga + baki/baaki = outstanding amount held by that party
sanga can mean WITH (joint) or FROM (informal purchase)
X sanga paise chha: X currently holds money (not necessarily owes)
saadh sanga = 'with' in settlement context
common_confusions: sanga vs bata for purchases: 'Ram sanga kineko' (informal) = 'Ram bata kineko' (formal)
sita is high-register synonym of sanga
sanga misread as possessive: 'mero sanga' = I have (possession, not transfer)

example_patterns.meaning: X's money / money belonging to X

disambiguation_rules: ko before bill/hisab/baki = subject who owes or is owed
ko alone does not imply direction — needs verb for direction
person ko baki = that person's outstanding (receivable for you)
period ko hisab = date-scoped transaction filter
ko + amount = 'worth/of amount': '5000 ko maal' = goods worth 5000
common_confusions: ko vs lai: 'Ram ko bill' = Ram's bill; 'Ram lai bill' = give bill to Ram
'5000 ko' means 'worth 5000' not 'from 5000'
ko can nominalise: 'becheko ko paisa' = money from what was sold

example_patterns.meaning: entered in account / transferred to account

disambiguation_rules: ma + jamma/haalnu = deposit target (inbound to that account/place)
ma + becheko = sale location (not direction of money)
ma + date = temporal anchor for transaction
account ma = transfer to account (inbound to account)
ma alone is neutral — needs verb to determine direction
common_confusions: ma vs lai for accounts: 'account ma' vs 'account lai' both used for deposit
temporal ma: 'June ma' = in June (not a place)
'kharcha ma' = in expenses (categorisation, not location)

example_patterns.meaning: no payment has come from X since

disambiguation_rules: dekhi = FROM (time); bata = FROM (place/source)
dekhi always marks the start of a range, not a source of funds
dekhi + date + samma = full time range for reports
dekhi alone can signal overdue calculation start
common_confusions: dekhi vs bata: 'bank bata' = from bank (source); 'Falgun dekhi' = from Falgun (time)
dekhera (from dekhnu = to see) is unrelated homophone
deki is common misspelling but same meaning

grammatical_role: terminative / limit marker (until / up to / as far as)

example_patterns.meaning: up to how much to give?

disambiguation_rules: samma + date = deadline or reporting end date
samma + amount = ceiling/limit on credit or spending
dekhi...samma = from...to (range) for period reports
aaja samma = as of today (used in outstanding calculations)
samma does not imply direction — context verb required
common_confusions: samm/sam are colloquial short forms
'samma' after amount = 'up to that amount' (ceiling, not total)
sometimes used loosely as 'about': '5000 samma' ≈ 'around 5000'

grammatical_role: purposive / benefactive marker (for / in order to)
disambiguation_rules: lagi = 'for purpose of'; not same as lai (recipient)
lagi after project/reason = expense categorisation signal
X lagi = for X's benefit (beneficiary, not recipient of cash)
lagi + advance = purpose of the advance payment
lagi vs lai: 'Ram lagi' = for Ram's benefit; 'Ram lai' = to Ram directly
common_confusions: lagi and lai both translate as 'for/to' in English
'mero lagi' = for me (purpose); 'malai' = to me (recipient)
laagi is extended form, same meaning

disambiguation_rules: prati + unit = per-unit rate calculation signal
prati + time = periodic rate (rent, salary, EMI)
prati + person (formal) = addressee of formal document
prati alone before amount without unit = formal 'towards'
rate context: prati is strong signal to extract unit price
common_confusions: prati vs lai for formal letters: both used as 'to'
priti is colloquial mispronunciation
'parti' spelling common in typed Romanized Nepali

disambiguation_rules: bina = without; flags missing document or step in transaction
bina + bill/receipt = undocumented transaction (compliance risk)
bina + approval = unauthorized transaction signal
bina is a negative dependency marker — what was absent
critical for audit/compliance detection in transaction logs
common_confusions: bina vs bahek: bina = without (absent element); bahek = except (excluded member of set)
vina is formal Sanskrit variant, same meaning
binna is a frequent misspelling

example_patterns.pattern: X bahek sabai items stock ma
example_patterns.meaning: all items in stock except X

disambiguation_rules: bahek = except; marks what is NOT included in the transaction/set
VAT bahek = exclusive of tax (important for amount parsing)
bahek + party = excluded payer/payee
bahek is set-exclusion; bina is element-absence — different semantics
use bahek to identify carve-outs from totals
common_confusions: bahek vs bina: 'receipt bina' = without receipt; 'Ram bahek' = except Ram
baahek extended spelling same meaning
colloquially sometimes used as 'other than' with loose logic

grammatical_role: comparative marker (than / more than / less than)
disambiguation_rules: bhanda + badi/beshi = above threshold (filter signal)
bhanda + ghati/kami = below threshold
bhanda is always comparative; not a transfer direction marker
bhanda pairs with badi/ghati/beshi/kami to form comparisons
use bhanda to detect threshold filters in queries: 'show me amounts > X'
common_confusions: vanda is colloquial Romanization
bhanta is misspelling
bhanda (than) vs bhannu (to say) — different words, same root confusion in text

example_patterns.meaning: it seems like it was sold

disambiguation_rules: jasto + amount = treat as approximate, not exact figure
jasto = similarity hedge; reduces confidence in exact value extraction
jasto chhaina = doesn't match (reconciliation mismatch signal)
lagyo + jasto = epistemic uncertainty ('seems like')
parser should flag jasto-modified amounts as approximate
common_confusions: jasto vs jati: jasto = like/similar; jati = as much as
jestho is a misspelling (also homophone of 'elder' in some dialects)
jasto chhaina ≠ not like; context-dependent negation

disambiguation_rules: pachhadi + event = payment/action contingent on prior event
pachhadi + deadline = late payment indicator
pachhadi is temporal sequencing — does not mark direction of money
pachi is short form, same meaning
pair with verb to determine if payment is late or conditional
common_confusions: pachhadi vs agaadi: pachhadi = after/behind; agaadi = before/ahead
pachi is very common short form
pachhi also used — same meaning

example_patterns.meaning: payment has been made in advance

disambiguation_rules: agaadi + payment = advance or prepayment
agaadi marks precondition — action before another event
agaadi vs pachhadi: agaadi = before; pachhadi = after
aghi is shorter near-synonym, very common in speech
pehile is another synonym (before, earlier)
common_confusions: agaadi vs aghi: same meaning, aghi more colloquial
pehile preferred in temporal 'earlier' sense
agaadi can be spatial (in front of) or temporal (before)

grammatical_role: locative / amount threshold (above / over / on top)

example_patterns.meaning: bonus on top of basic salary

disambiguation_rules: mathi + amount = above that amount (filter threshold)
mathi = additional/on top of (stacking charges)
mathi bata = from above (hierarchy, not amount)
amount mathi = exceeds amount (overrun signal)
mathi vs tala: mathi = above/over; tala = below/under
common_confusions: mati is frequent misspelling (also means 'soil' in some dialects)
mathi (above) vs mathi (head office direction) — context dependent
spatial vs amount sense must be disambiguated by context

disambiguation_rules: tala + amount = below that amount (lower bound filter)
tala = under/below in both spatial and amount senses
tala bata = from below (branch/subordinate)
tala + stock = reorder alert signal
tala vs mathi: tala = below; mathi = above
common_confusions: tal is short form — same meaning
tala (below) vs tala (floor/lock) — homophones in different contexts
taala means 'lock' — do not confuse with tala (below)

disambiguation_rules: bhitra + time = deadline (must complete within period)
bhitra + amount = within budget (spending constraint)
bhitra + location = geographic delivery scope
bhitra is always inclusional — inside a boundary
bhitra vs baahira: bhitra = within; baahira = outside
common_confusions: bitra is common misspelling
bhitri = interior (adjective form, not postposition)
bhitra (within) vs bhitra (inside building) — same word, context determines scope

example_patterns.meaning: extra charge for delivery outside Kathmandu

disambiguation_rules: baahira + location = out-of-zone (surcharge possible)
baahira + budget = overspend signal
baahira + scope = out-of-scope (change order needed)
Nepal baahira = international transaction / export
baahira vs bhitra: baahira = outside boundary; bhitra = inside boundary
common_confusions: bahira is common short spelling
baahira (outside) vs baahira (going out) — same root
VAT baahira = VAT exempt, not 'sent outside for VAT'

example_patterns.meaning: send near my house / to my area

disambiguation_rules: nira = near (spatial); almost never marks money direction
nira used in delivery address specification
nira is low-signal for financial transactions; flag as location only
nira vs najik: same meaning; najik more common in formal writing
parser: nira → extract location entity, not transaction direction
common_confusions: nira (near) vs nira (name) — common Nepali name
neer is Romanized misspelling
confuse with nira (name) in customer name fields

example_patterns.meaning: higher delivery charge for distant location

disambiguation_rules: tadha = far; implies higher logistics cost
tadha bata = from a distant source (freight cost signal)
tadha delivery = flag for distance-based pricing
tadha vs najik: tadha = far; najik = near
parser: tadha → flag for transport surcharge lookup
common_confusions: taadha is common extended spelling
taaDha is Unicode-transliteration form
tadha (far) unrelated to tadha in some dialects meaning 'then'

example_patterns.pattern: Ram ra Shyam bich ko hisab

example_patterns.meaning: took commission in between / as middleman

disambiguation_rules: bich = between two parties or two amounts
bich ma = in between / in the middle (middleman signal)
date bich = date range for filtering
bich ko hisab = inter-party account requiring two-sided entry
bich commission = intermediary fee
common_confusions: beech is English-influenced spelling
bich (between) vs beech (tree in English)
bichh/bicch are common typos

grammatical_role: proximal side marker (this side / on our side)

example_patterns.meaning: how much stock is on our side
delivery on this side of the river

disambiguation_rules: wari = this side / our side (near side of boundary)
wari vs pari: wari = near side; pari = far/other side
wari bata = from our side (we are sending/giving)
wari ko = belonging to / on our side
in trade: wari = domestic/local; pari = across border
common_confusions: wari (this side) vs wari (regarding — archaic usage)
vari is alternate spelling
waari is extended Romanization

example_patterns.pattern: pari ko party bata payment aayena
example_patterns.meaning: payment has not come from the other party
sent to account on the other side

example_patterns.pattern: pari ra wari ko hisab milaaunu

example_patterns.meaning: the other side hasn't sent money

disambiguation_rules: pari = other side / counterparty
pari vs wari: pari = their side; wari = our side
pari bata = from the other party (inbound expected)
pari ko = belonging to counterparty / cross-border
pari + payment pending = accounts receivable from counterparty
common_confusions: pari (other side) vs pari (fairy — English loanword in Nepali)
paari is extended spelling
pari bata vs pari ko: source vs ownership distinction

example_patterns.meaning: might need to go towards bank

disambiguation_rules: tira = approximate direction (less precise than lai)
tira after person/place = towards, not definitive recipient
tira after time = approximately at that time
payment tira = payment direction (weaker than payment lai)
tira signals approximation — extract with lower confidence
common_confusions: tira (towards) vs tirnu (to pay) — different words, easy confusion in short text
tir is short form of tira (same meaning)
tira and lai sometimes interchanged: 'X tira deko' ≈ 'X lai deko'

example_patterns.meaning: payment almost due / close to due date

disambiguation_rules: najik = near (spatial) or approaching (temporal)
najik + deadline = urgency signal for payment
najik ko branch = nearest service point
najik aako = approaching (temporal, deadline alert)
najik vs nira: both mean near; najik more formal/common
common_confusions: najik (near) used temporally: 'due date najik cha' = deadline approaching
najeek is Romanized colloquial form
najik aako payment = payment approaching (not payment nearby)

disambiguation_rules: muni = under authority, not physical 'below'
muni + tax = category/classification signal
muni + budget = within a budget line item
muni + person = hierarchical subordination
muni vs tala: muni = under authority/category; tala = physically below or numerically lower
common_confusions: muni (under) vs Muni (sage/saint — proper noun)
mooni is colloquial Romanization
muni and tala both translate as 'under' but different uses

grammatical_role: super-locative / additional charge marker (over / upon / on top of)

example_patterns.meaning: 13% VAT on top of base amount

disambiguation_rules: upara = on top of / over (stacking charges)
upara + percentage = tax/surcharge rate applied to base
udharo upara byaaj = compound interest signal
upara vs mathi: upara more formal/legal; mathi more colloquial
upara marks what is added ON TOP of a base figure
common_confusions: upara and mathi are near synonyms: upara more formal, mathi more conversational
upar is short form (North Indian influence)
upara (on top) vs upari (adjective: upper)

disambiguation_rules: aghi = prior to / earlier than (temporal only)
aghi ko = previous / prior (references historical record)
aghi deko = given previously (look up prior entry)
aghi vs agaadi: aghi = prior (already happened); agaadi = ahead (hasn't happened)
aghi signals need to look up historical transaction records
common_confusions: aghi vs agaadi: aghi = earlier (past reference); agaadi = in front / before (future or spatial)
agi is common misspelling
aghai is emphatic form: 'aghai deko thiyo' = had given even earlier
